title: Declarations, prototypes and definitions

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Declarations, prototypes and definitions; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The header declaration double average(const int *values, size_t count) is visible at every call and matches one external definition. Your goal is to design, declare, define and call a type-correct function. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: while, do and for; pf abstraction calls; Scalar types and declarations; pf abstraction functions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A function prototype declares return and parameter types so calls can be checked and arguments converted according to the declared interface before the definition is seen. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Old-style unspecified parameter lists differ from a void parameter list, variadic arguments receive less checking, and incompatible declarations across units make behavior undefined. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Writing reset() in a C declaration always means the function takes no arguments, exactly like reset(void).

includes: Declare functions before use and keep interfaces compatible with definitions.
Design, declare, define and call a type-correct function.
excludes: Old-style unspecified parameter lists differ from a void parameter list, variadic arguments receive less checking, and incompatible declarations across units make behavior undefined.

outcomes: Design, declare, define and call a type-correct function.

examples.title: Declarations, prototypes and definitions: defined case
examples.situation: The header declaration double average(const int *values, size_t count) is visible at every call and matches one external definition.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: The header declaration double average(const int *values, size_t count) is visible at every call and matches one external definition.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: In a C prototype, use (void) to declare no parameters; an empty parameter list does not provide the same prototype information.
examples.result: The defined result follows because In a C prototype, use (void) to declare no parameters; an empty parameter list does not provide the same prototype information.

examples.title: Declarations, prototypes and definitions: boundary case
examples.situation: A no-argument function is declared reset(void), explicitly stating that it accepts no parameters in C.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A no-argument function is declared reset(void), explicitly stating that it accepts no parameters in C.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Old-style unspecified parameter lists differ from a void parameter list, variadic arguments receive less checking, and incompatible declarations across units make behavior undefined.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Writing reset() in a C declaration always means the function takes no arguments, exactly like reset(void).
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design, declare, define and call a type-correct function.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Writing reset() in a C declaration always means the function takes no arguments, exactly like reset(void).
misconceptions.correction: In a C prototype, use (void) to declare no parameters; an empty parameter list does not provide the same prototype information.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Writing reset() in a C declaration always means the function takes no arguments, exactly like reset(void).

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Declarations, prototypes and definitions: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Old-style unspecified parameter lists differ from a void parameter list, variadic arguments receive less checking, and incompatible declarations across units make behavior undefined.
